black-necked-stilt.size: Medium shorebird, about 35–40 cm long with extremely long pink legs
black-necked-stilt.habitat: Wetlands, coastlines, marshes, and shallow ponds of North and South America
black-necked-stilt.diet: Aquatic invertebrates, insects, and small fish probed from shallow water
black-necked-stilt.migration: Northern inland populations migrate south in winter toward southern Mexico; coastal and southern populations are largely resident

great-blue-heron.size: Large wading bird, one of North America's tallest birds at roughly 100–130 cm
great-blue-heron.habitat: Shores of open water and wetlands across most of North and Central America
great-blue-heron.diet: Primarily fish, supplemented by frogs, small mammals, and other aquatic prey
great-blue-heron.migration: Largely resident across most of range; northern populations move south in winter

great-egret.size: Large egret, roughly 80–104 cm long with a wingspan around 130–170 cm
great-egret.habitat: Wetlands, marshes, lakes, rivers, and coastal areas across tropical and warmer temperate regions worldwide
great-egret.diet: Fish, frogs, small mammals, and other aquatic prey stalked in shallow water
great-egret.migration: Partly migratory; northern populations move south in winter, southern populations largely resident

snowy-egret.size: Small white heron, noticeably smaller than the great egret; about 56–66 cm long
snowy-egret.habitat: Wetlands, marshes, tidal flats, ponds, and coastal areas of the Americas
snowy-egret.diet: Small fish, crustaceans, frogs, and aquatic invertebrates
snowy-egret.migration: Migratory in northern parts of range; winters along coasts and in southern regions

green-heron.size: Small heron of North and Central America, about 41–46 cm long
green-heron.habitat: Wooded streams, ponds, marshes, and vegetated shorelines of North and Central America
green-heron.diet: Fish, frogs, and aquatic invertebrates; notably uses bait to lure prey
green-heron.migration: Migratory; northern populations winter in Central America and the Caribbean

black-crowned-night-heron.size: Medium-sized heron, about 58–66 cm long with a stocky, compact build
black-crowned-night-heron.habitat: Wetlands, marshes, estuaries, and lakeshores across much of Europe, Asia, and the Americas
black-crowned-night-heron.diet: Fish, frogs, crustaceans, insects, and other small aquatic animals; primarily a nocturnal hunter
black-crowned-night-heron.migration: Partly migratory; northern populations move south in winter, many populations are resident year-round

american-white-pelican.size: Very large aquatic bird; among the largest in North America by wingspan
american-white-pelican.habitat: Lakes, marshes, and rivers in interior North America; coastal areas in winter
american-white-pelican.diet: Primarily fish, caught cooperatively by herding prey in shallow water
american-white-pelican.migration: Strongly migratory; breeds in interior North America, winters south to the coasts and as far as Costa Rica

brown-pelican.size: Large coastal bird, about 100–137 cm long with a wingspan up to about 2.3 m
brown-pelican.habitat: Coastal ocean waters, bays, estuaries, and beaches from New Jersey to South America
brown-pelican.diet: Primarily fish, caught by dramatic plunge-diving; occasionally amphibians and crustaceans
brown-pelican.migration: Largely resident along coasts; some post-breeding dispersal northward along both coasts

double-crested-cormorant.size: 70–90 cm long; entirely black with an orange-yellow facial patch
double-crested-cormorant.habitat: Rivers, lakes, and coastal areas across North America from Alaska to Florida and Mexico
double-crested-cormorant.diet: Mainly fish, hunted by swimming and diving underwater
double-crested-cormorant.migration: Partly migratory; northern populations move south in winter, coastal populations often resident

sandhill-crane.size: Large crane of North America, standing roughly 80–120 cm tall with a broad wingspan
sandhill-crane.habitat: Open wetlands, meadows, marshes, and the edges of bodies of water across North America
sandhill-crane.diet: Omnivorous; grains, tubers, insects, small vertebrates, and other plant and animal matter
sandhill-crane.migration: Strongly migratory; up to 450,000 birds stage along the Platte River in Nebraska each spring

american-coot.size: Medium-sized waterbird, roughly the size of a small duck; about 34–43 cm long
american-coot.habitat: Wetlands, ponds, lakes, and open water across North America
american-coot.diet: Primarily algae and aquatic plants; also invertebrates and small vertebrates when available
american-coot.migration: Migratory; breeds across North America, winters from the southern US south to Panama

common-gallinule.size: Medium-sized rail, similar in size to a small duck; about 30–38 cm long
common-gallinule.habitat: Well-vegetated marshes, ponds, canals, and other wetlands across the Americas
common-gallinule.diet: Seeds, aquatic plants, invertebrates, and small fish gleaned from the water's edge
common-gallinule.migration: Partly migratory; northern populations move south in winter, many populations resident year-round

pied-billed-grebe.size: Small, compact waterbird; about 30–38 cm long
pied-billed-grebe.habitat: Ponds, lakes, and freshwater marshes throughout the Americas
pied-billed-grebe.diet: Fish, crustaceans, aquatic insects, and other small aquatic animals caught by diving
pied-billed-grebe.migration: Partly migratory; northern populations move south in winter to open water

wood-stork.size: Large wading bird, about 83–115 cm long; the only stork that breeds in North America
wood-stork.habitat: Subtropical and tropical wetlands, swamps, and areas with fluctuating water levels in the Americas
wood-stork.diet: Fish and other aquatic prey, caught by tactile bill-snapping in shallow water
wood-stork.migration: Largely resident in tropical and subtropical range; US population disperses after breeding

roseate-spoonbill.size: Large wading bird, about 71–86 cm long with a distinctive spatula-shaped bill
roseate-spoonbill.habitat: Coastal wetlands, mangroves, marshes, and shallow bays in South and North America
roseate-spoonbill.diet: Small fish, crustaceans, aquatic insects, and other invertebrates sifted by sweeping the bill
roseate-spoonbill.migration: Largely resident; some post-breeding dispersal northward along Gulf and Atlantic coasts

white-ibis.size: Medium-sized wading bird, about 53–70 cm long with a long downward-curving red-orange bill
white-ibis.habitat: Coastal wetlands, mangroves, marshes, and shallow water along the US Gulf and Atlantic coasts south through Central America
white-ibis.diet: Primarily crayfish and small aquatic prey, probed by feel in shallow water; also insects and small fish
white-ibis.migration: Largely resident; range extends inland after the breeding season and into the Caribbean

trumpeter-swan.size: The largest extant waterfowl species; wingspan 185–305 cm; the heaviest bird native to North America
trumpeter-swan.habitat: Lakes, rivers, and wetlands with aquatic vegetation in North America
trumpeter-swan.diet: Aquatic plants, roots, and tubers; also some aquatic invertebrates
trumpeter-swan.migration: Partly migratory; many populations resident year-round, others move to ice-free waters in winter

tundra-swan.size: The smallest Holarctic swan; medium-large waterfowl with white plumage and mostly black bill
tundra-swan.habitat: Breeds on Arctic and subarctic tundra pools and lakes; winters in grasslands and marshes, often near coasts
tundra-swan.diet: Mainly aquatic vegetation in summer; leftover grain and crops in winter
tundra-swan.migration: Strongly migratory; breeds in Arctic tundra and winters on coasts of North America and northwestern Europe

snow-goose.size: Medium-sized goose; smaller than Canada goose, with both white and dark ('blue') color morphs
snow-goose.habitat: Breeds on Arctic tundra; winters in agricultural fields, wetlands, and coastal marshes of North America
snow-goose.diet: Grasses, sedges, agricultural grain, and other plant matter
snow-goose.migration: Strongly migratory; breeds in Greenland, Canada, and Alaska; winters in warm parts of North America south to Mexico

northern-pintail.size: Large dabbling duck; males have elongated central tail feathers giving them a distinctively slender profile
northern-pintail.habitat: Open wetlands, lakes, marshes, and agricultural fields across northern Europe, Asia, and North America
northern-pintail.diet: Primarily plant matter dabbled from shallow water; adds small invertebrates during breeding season
northern-pintail.migration: Strongly migratory; breeds in northern areas, winters south to the equator

american-wigeon.size: Medium-sized dabbling duck, similar in size to a mallard
american-wigeon.habitat: Wetlands, ponds, lakes, and estuaries across North America
american-wigeon.diet: Primarily aquatic vegetation and grasses; often grazes on land near water
american-wigeon.migration: Migratory; breeds in northern North America, winters along coasts and in southern regions

northern-shoveler.size: Medium-sized dabbling duck with a distinctive large spatula-shaped bill
northern-shoveler.habitat: Wetlands, shallow lakes, and marshes; breeds in northern Europe and North America
northern-shoveler.diet: Aquatic invertebrates, seeds, and plant matter filtered through its large bill
northern-shoveler.migration: Strongly migratory; winters in southern Europe, South Asia, Central America, and northern South America

green-winged-teal.size: One of the smallest North American dabbling ducks
green-winged-teal.habitat: Ponds, marshes, and wetlands across northern North America
green-winged-teal.diet: Seeds, aquatic plants, and small invertebrates dabbled in shallow water
green-winged-teal.migration: Strongly migratory; winters far south of its breeding range, forming large gregarious flocks

blue-winged-teal.size: One of the smaller dabbling ducks of North America
blue-winged-teal.habitat: Freshwater ponds, marshes, and shallow lakes; breeds from southern Alaska to Nova Scotia
blue-winged-teal.diet: Seeds, aquatic plants, and invertebrates dabbled in shallow water
blue-winged-teal.migration: Long-distance migrant; winters along Pacific and Atlantic coasts, into the Caribbean and Central America

gadwall.size: Medium-sized dabbling duck, slightly smaller than a mallard
gadwall.habitat: Wetlands, lakes, ponds, and marshes across the Northern Hemisphere
gadwall.migration: Migratory; breeds in northern regions, winters in southern and coastal areas

canvasback.size: The largest diving duck in North America
canvasback.habitat: Prairie potholes, lakes, and wetlands for breeding; large open lakes and coastal bays in winter
canvasback.diet: Primarily aquatic plant tubers (especially wild celery), supplemented by invertebrates
canvasback.migration: Migratory; breeds in prairie pothole region, winters on large coastal bays and inland lakes

redhead.size: 40–56 cm long; wingspan 74–84 cm; weight around 1,030–1,080 g
redhead.habitat: Prairie wetlands and marshes for breeding; large lakes and coastal bays in winter
redhead.diet: Aquatic plants, seeds, and invertebrates, obtained by diving
redhead.migration: Migratory; breeds in prairie pothole region, winters on coastal bays and large inland lakes

ring-necked-duck.size: Medium-sized diving duck of North America
ring-necked-duck.habitat: Freshwater ponds and wooded lakes; breeds in boreal forest wetlands, winters on ponds and reservoirs
ring-necked-duck.diet: Aquatic plants, seeds, and invertebrates obtained by diving
ring-necked-duck.migration: Migratory; breeds in northern North America, winters across the southern US and into Central America

bufflehead.size: Small sea duck; one of the smallest ducks in North America
bufflehead.habitat: Wooded lakes and ponds for breeding in Alaska and Canada; coastal bays and open water in winter
bufflehead.diet: Aquatic invertebrates, insects, and small crustaceans obtained by diving
bufflehead.migration: Migratory; breeds in Alaska and Canada, winters across southern North America

common-goldeneye.size: Medium-sized sea duck with a distinctive bulbous head profile
common-goldeneye.habitat: Forested lakes and rivers for breeding; coastal bays and large lakes in winter
common-goldeneye.diet: Aquatic invertebrates, crustaceans, and small fish obtained by diving
common-goldeneye.migration: Migratory; breeds in boreal forests of North America and Eurasia, winters on coasts and large inland waters

hooded-merganser.size: The second-smallest merganser species; a small fish-eating duck with a prominent erectile crest
hooded-merganser.habitat: Wooded wetlands, ponds, and rivers in North America
hooded-merganser.diet: Primarily fish, also crayfish and aquatic invertebrates, caught by diving
hooded-merganser.migration: Migratory; breeds in forested areas of North America, winters on ponds, estuaries, and coastal waters

common-merganser.size: Large sea duck; the largest merganser in North America and Eurasia
common-merganser.habitat: Rivers and lakes in forested areas of Europe, Asia, and North America
common-merganser.diet: Mainly fish, caught by diving in clear freshwater rivers and lakes
common-merganser.migration: Partly migratory; northern populations move south in winter to ice-free rivers and coastal waters

ruddy-duck.size: Small, compact stiff-tailed duck with a short, stout body
ruddy-duck.habitat: Prairie potholes and marshes for breeding; coastal bays and large lakes in winter
ruddy-duck.diet: Aquatic invertebrates and plant material obtained by diving
ruddy-duck.migration: Migratory; breeds in prairie wetlands of North America, winters on coastal bays and open water

golden-eagle.size: Large raptor; dark brown with golden-buff nape; may maintain home ranges up to 200 km²
golden-eagle.habitat: Open and semi-open country, mountains, and cliffs across the Holarctic; avoids densely human-populated areas
golden-eagle.diet: Mainly hares, rabbits, marmots, and ground squirrels, taken with powerful talons
golden-eagle.migration: Largely resident; some northern populations move south in winter; juveniles wander widely until establishing a territory

coopers-hawk.size: Medium-sized hawk; females noticeably larger than males; crow-sized to pigeon-sized depending on sex
coopers-hawk.habitat: Forests and woodlands across North America from southern Canada to Mexico; increasingly common in suburbs
coopers-hawk.diet: Primarily small to medium-sized birds; also small mammals and occasionally reptiles
coopers-hawk.migration: Partly migratory; northern populations move south in autumn, many populations resident year-round

sharp-shinned-hawk.size: Small hawk; males are the smallest hawks in the United States and Canada
sharp-shinned-hawk.habitat: Dense forests and woodlands across North America; also found in thickets and forest edges
sharp-shinned-hawk.diet: Primarily small birds caught in fast, agile pursuit through forest undergrowth
sharp-shinned-hawk.migration: Strongly migratory; one of the most numerous hawks seen at migration watchsites in autumn

northern-harrier.size: Medium-sized, slender hawk with a long tail and long wings adapted for low soaring
northern-harrier.habitat: Prairies, open areas, and marshes across North America
northern-harrier.diet: Small mammals, birds, and frogs hunted by flying low over open ground
northern-harrier.migration: Migratory; breeds in northern Canada and US, winters south to the American south, Mexico, and Central America

american-kestrel.size: The smallest falcon in the Americas; ranges from about the weight of a blue jay to a mourning dove
american-kestrel.habitat: Open country, farmland, grasslands, and urban areas throughout North and South America
american-kestrel.diet: Grasshoppers and other insects, lizards, mice, and small birds
american-kestrel.migration: Partly migratory; Canadian and northern US breeders move south in winter; southern populations resident

peregrine-falcon.size: Large, crow-sized falcon with blue-grey back, barred white underparts, and a black head
peregrine-falcon.habitat: Nearly worldwide — cliff faces, urban buildings, tundra, and coastlines on every continent except Antarctica
peregrine-falcon.diet: Almost exclusively medium-sized birds caught in high-speed aerial dives; pigeons are a key urban prey
peregrine-falcon.migration: Many northern populations are migratory, with the scientific name meaning 'wandering falcon'; urban populations largely resident

merlin.size: Small, compact falcon; slightly larger than an American kestrel
merlin.habitat: Open forests, prairies, and coastlines across the Northern Hemisphere
merlin.diet: Primarily small birds caught in fast, low pursuit; also large insects
merlin.migration: Migratory; breeds in boreal forests and tundra of Canada and northern US, winters across southern North America and into South America

turkey-vulture.size: Large soaring bird; wingspan roughly 160–183 cm; holds wings in a shallow V in flight
turkey-vulture.habitat: Open and semi-open areas including subtropical forests, shrublands, pastures, and deserts from Canada to South America
turkey-vulture.diet: Almost exclusively carrion, located by an exceptional sense of smell; uses thermals to soar in search of carcasses
turkey-vulture.migration: Migratory in northern range; US birds legally protected under the Migratory Bird Treaty Act; winters in warmer regions

black-vulture.size: Wingspan about 1.5 m (4.9 ft); relatively small for a vulture with black plumage and a bare grayish-black head
black-vulture.habitat: Open areas with scattered trees, chaparral, subtropical forests, and garbage dumps from southeastern US to South America
black-vulture.diet: Primarily carrion; also eggs, small reptiles, and newborn animals; finds food mainly by sight, following other vultures
black-vulture.migration: Largely resident throughout its range from the southeastern US to South America

eastern-screech-owl.size: Stocky, short-tailed owl; mid-sized for a screech owl
eastern-screech-owl.habitat: Most types of woodland habitats in eastern North America, from Mexico to Canada; adapts to urban and suburban areas
eastern-screech-owl.diet: Small mammals, birds, and large insects hunted nocturnally; adaptable opportunistic predator

barred-owl.size: Large owl species of North America, brown to gray overall with dark striping on the underside
barred-owl.habitat: Mature forests of eastern North America; has expanded west and is now found along the Pacific Coast
barred-owl.diet: Small mammals primarily; opportunistically takes birds, reptiles, amphibians, and invertebrates

burrowing-owl.size: Small, long-legged owl; distinctive among owls for its terrestrial lifestyle
burrowing-owl.habitat: Grasslands, rangelands, agricultural areas, and deserts with low vegetation in North and South America
burrowing-owl.diet: Insects, small mammals, and other small prey; often active during the day, hunting at dawn and dusk
burrowing-owl.migration: Partly migratory; northern populations move south in winter, southern populations largely resident

ruffed-grouse.size: Medium-sized grouse; the most widely distributed game bird in North America
ruffed-grouse.habitat: Forests from the Appalachian Mountains across Canada to Alaska
ruffed-grouse.diet: Buds, leaves, berries, seeds, and insects; diet shifts seasonally with plant availability
ruffed-grouse.migration: Non-migratory; resident year-round in its forest habitat

northern-bobwhite.size: Small, compact ground-dwelling quail of eastern North America
northern-bobwhite.habitat: Open woodlands, grasslands, agricultural edges, and brushy areas of Canada, the US, Mexico, and Cuba
northern-bobwhite.diet: Seeds, insects, and plant matter foraged from the ground
northern-bobwhite.migration: Largely resident; does not typically migrate, though populations have declined sharply due to habitat loss

ring-necked-pheasant.size: Large, long-tailed gamebird native to Asia; introduced widely as a game bird
ring-necked-pheasant.habitat: Farmlands, grasslands, forest edges, and open countryside; highly adaptable to disturbed habitats
ring-necked-pheasant.diet: Seeds, grains, berries, insects, and plant matter; forages mostly on the ground
ring-necked-pheasant.migration: Largely resident; does not undertake regular migrations

greater-roadrunner.size: Long-legged, long-tailed bird of the Southwestern US and Mexico; one of the larger members of the cuckoo family
greater-roadrunner.habitat: Arid and semi-arid scrubland, chaparral, desert, and open woodland of the Southwestern US and Mexico
greater-roadrunner.diet: Snakes (including rattlesnakes), lizards, large insects, small mammals, and birds
greater-roadrunner.migration: Non-migratory; resident year-round in its arid range

yellow-billed-cuckoo.size: Medium-sized, slender songbird with a long tail; somewhat larger than a robin
yellow-billed-cuckoo.habitat: Riparian woodlands, forest edges, and thickets in eastern North America during summer
yellow-billed-cuckoo.diet: Large caterpillars (especially tent caterpillars and fall webworms), cicadas, and other large insects
yellow-billed-cuckoo.migration: Long-distance migrant; breeds in North America and winters in South America

common-nighthawk.size: 22–25 cm long; wingspan 51–61 cm; weight 55–98 g
common-nighthawk.habitat: Open areas, grasslands, urban rooftops, and woodland edges across the Americas
common-nighthawk.diet: Aerial insectivore; catches flying insects in its enormous gaping mouth during crepuscular and nocturnal flights
common-nighthawk.migration: Long-distance migrant; breeds across North America, winters in South America

acorn-woodpecker.size: About 20 cm (8 in) long; average weight about 85 g (3.0 oz)
acorn-woodpecker.habitat: Oak woodlands and pine-oak forests of western North America and Central America, south into Colombia
acorn-woodpecker.diet: Acorns are the staple, stored communally in 'granary trees'; also insects and fruit
acorn-woodpecker.migration: Non-migratory; resident year-round in its oak woodland habitat

stellers-jay.size: Medium-sized jay; the only crested jay west of the Rocky Mountains
stellers-jay.habitat: Pine-oak and coniferous forests of western North America and mountains of Central America
stellers-jay.diet: Seeds, nuts, berries, insects, and occasionally eggs or nestlings of other birds
stellers-jay.migration: Largely resident; some elevational movement in winter

western-scrub-jay.size: Medium-sized, slender jay without a crest
western-scrub-jay.habitat: Scrub, chaparral, oak woodland, and urban/suburban areas in western North America from British Columbia to Nevada and California
western-scrub-jay.diet: Acorns, seeds, insects, berries, and small vertebrates; also caches food for winter
western-scrub-jay.migration: Non-migratory; resident year-round throughout its range

clarks-nutcracker.size: Medium-sized corvid; gray-bodied with black-and-white wings
clarks-nutcracker.habitat: Mountain forests of western North America, primarily associated with high-elevation pine forests
clarks-nutcracker.diet: Pine seeds (its primary food); stores thousands of seeds in the ground for winter and can relocate them by memory
clarks-nutcracker.migration: Largely resident at high elevations; irrupts to lower elevations in years of pine seed failure

black-billed-magpie.size: Medium-large corvid; black and white with iridescent blue-green wing and tail sheen; long tail
black-billed-magpie.habitat: Open habitats with clumps of trees, farmlands, and suburban areas across the western half of North America
black-billed-magpie.diet: Omnivorous — beetles, grasshoppers, worms, rodents, carrion, ticks gleaned from livestock, and berries
black-billed-magpie.migration: Non-migratory; resident year-round throughout its range

american-dipper.size: Small, compact, wren-like waterbird; the only truly aquatic songbird in North America
american-dipper.habitat: Fast-flowing, clear mountain streams and rivers of western North America
american-dipper.diet: Aquatic invertebrates, fish eggs, and small fish found by walking underwater along stream bottoms
american-dipper.migration: Largely resident; may move to lower elevations or less-frozen streams in severe winters

varied-thrush.habitat: Dense coniferous and mixed forests of the Pacific Coast and mountain ranges of western North America
varied-thrush.diet: Berries, acorns, and other fruit in winter; insects and earthworms in breeding season
varied-thrush.migration: Partly migratory; moves from higher-elevation breeding areas to lower coastal areas in winter

western-tanager.size: Medium-sized songbird; male is brilliantly colored with a red head and yellow body
western-tanager.habitat: Coniferous and mixed forests of western North America during breeding; tropical forests and woodland edges in winter
western-tanager.diet: Insects (especially wasps and bees) during breeding; berries and fruit supplement the diet
western-tanager.migration: Long-distance migrant; breeds in western North America, winters from Mexico to Costa Rica

lazuli-bunting.size: Small songbird of western North America; named for the gemstone lapis lazuli
lazuli-bunting.habitat: Shrubby hillsides, streamside thickets, and chaparral of western North America
lazuli-bunting.diet: Seeds and insects; forages in low shrubs and on the ground
lazuli-bunting.migration: Migratory; breeds in western North America, winters in Mexico
